reason: Both answers correctly identify exactly-once delivery as impossible, distinguish it from idempotent processing, and describe the standard at-least-once plus dedup/idempotency plus transactional outbox approach, citing the same real-world examples (Kafka, SQS FIFO). They are very close in quality and correctness. B has a slight edge: it explicitly flags that non-idempotent external side effects (email, HTTP calls) still need their own handling even when internal processing is transactional, a practically important caveat that A omits. A's explicit naming of the Two Generals problem is a nice touch but doesn't outweigh B's added practical nuance. Overall the difference is marginal.
